### The Game Plan (Summary)

Phase 1: The Core Refactor (Extracting the BaseCanvas)Currently, your AnnotationWindow is a monolithic class handling both the viewing of images and the complex logic of drawing/editing annotations. We need to split this to make the viewports reusable.1. Create BaseCanvas (Inherits from QGraphicsView)Responsibilities:Initialize the QGraphicsScene and load the QPixmap (the image).Handle basic navigation events: zooming (mouse wheel) and panning (middle-click or right-click drag).Manage the display of visual overlays: the Static Cross Marker and the Dynamic Circle Marker.Expose generic methods like center_on_pixel(x, y) and set_zoom_level(factor).Maintain a list of read-only QGraphicsItem objects for visualizing annotations.2. Update AnnotationWindow (Inherits from BaseCanvas)Responsibilities:Everything related to creating or editing data.Managing the Action Stack (Undo/Redo).Handling specific Tool states (Polygon, SAM, Work Area, etc.).Emitting signals when annotations are changed or when the user moves the mouse.

Phase 2: Building the "Security Camera" MatrixThe current CameraGrid will be entirely replaced by a new ContextMatrixWidget.1. The Grid Chooser & Layout EngineReplace the scroll area with a flexible QGridLayout.Add a "Layout" dropdown menu to the toolbar with preset configurations: $1\times1$, $1\times2$, $2\times1$, $2\times2$, $3\times1$, etc.When a layout is selected (determining your $N$), the widget instantiates exactly $N$ BaseCanvas objects and slots them into the grid. Unused slots are hidden.2. Aspect Ratio Reactivity (Dock Placement)Hook into the dock's location-changed signal. If docked to the Left/Right, default to a vertical stack (e.g., $3\times1$). If docked to the Top/Bottom, switch to a horizontal strip (e.g., $1\times3$). Floating/Center gets a box grid (e.g., $2\times2$).

Phase 3: The Conveyor Belt Navigation Engine (NEW)To manage the $N$-nearest cameras efficiently without cluttering the UI, the ContextMatrixWidget acts as a "sliding window" over the full sorted list of cameras provided by the MVATManager.1. State ManagementThe widget maintains a current_rank_offset integer (defaulting to $0$, representing the 1st nearest camera).The widget listens for the specific hotkeys via the main window's event filter or its own key press events.2. The Micro-Step (Ctrl + Left/Right)Action: Shifts the current_rank_offset by $1$ or $-1$.Result: The matrix acts like a conveyor belt. If you have a $1\times3$ grid showing cameras ranked [1, 2, 3], pressing Ctrl + Right shifts the view to show cameras [2, 3, 4]. The images visually "slide" over one slot, dropping the least relevant and pulling in the next one.3. The Macro-Step / Paging (Ctrl + Up/Down)Action: Shifts the current_rank_offset by $N$ (the total number of visible canvases).Result: Pages the entire matrix. If showing [1, 2, 3], pressing Ctrl + Down immediately loads [4, 5, 6]. This allows the user to rapidly flip through tiers of the dataset.

Phase 4: Implementing the Dual-Marker SystemEach BaseCanvas needs to manage two distinct graphical items to map the 3D relationship.1. The Static Cross (MVAT Focal Point)When the user double-clicks to set a focal point in the MVATViewer, the 3D point is projected into every camera.The ContextMatrixWidget calls canvas.update_static_marker(x, y) for its active canvases. This marker remains glued to that pixel coordinate.2. The Dynamic Circle (Live Mouse Projection)When the mouse moves in the main AnnotationWindow, a ray is cast to the 3D world, and that point is projected into the context cameras.The ContextMatrixWidget calls canvas.update_dynamic_marker(x, y). This marker moves fluidly as the user scrubs their mouse.

Phase 5: The "Target-Locked" Sync EngineThis feature makes the conveyor belt feel magical. It relies on a "snap-back" architecture.1. The Sync TriggerThe AnnotationWindow emits a viewNavigated signal whenever the user pans or zooms.2. The Projection CalculationFind the exact center pixel of the AnnotationWindow's current view, cast a ray to 3D, and project that 3D coordinate into the $N$ visible context cameras to get their target 2D pixels.3. Snap-Back & Conveyor SynergyThe ContextMatrixWidget receives the targets and calls canvas.center_on_pixel(x, y) on each visible canvas, matching the relative zoom.The Synergy: Because the target lock is applied programmatically to the slots in the matrix, if the user hits Ctrl + Right to load the next camera on the conveyor belt, that new camera loads already zoomed and panned to the exact target area.The Override: If a user manually pans/zooms a specific context canvas, they can explore freely. The very next time they pan/zoom the main AnnotationWindow, all canvases instantly snap back to the synchronized target.

Phase 6: Annotation Visualization in Context ViewsBecause BaseCanvas is a fully functional QGraphicsView, seeing your work from multiple angles becomes straightforward.1. Decoupling Annotation StorageMove the master dictionary of annotations out of the AnnotationWindow and into a central manager (like MVATManager or a dedicated model).This allows any BaseCanvas to ask: "Give me all annotations for my assigned image."2. Real-Time RenderingWhen a BaseCanvas loads a new image off the conveyor belt, it retrieves that image's annotations, generates read-only QGraphicsItem representations, and adds them to its scene.If the user edits an annotation in the main window, a global annotationModified signal triggers the context canvases to redraw that specific item in real-time.
